title: Silicon-Carbon Bonds on Porous Silicon Surfaces
abstract:
Research News
Silicon±Carbon Bonds on Porous Silicon
Surfaces**
By Jillian M. Buriak*
Porous silicon has elicited intense interest for use in a wide
variety of optical, photodetector, photovoltaic, microma-
chine, and sensor applications since the discovery of its room
temperature photoluminescence (PL) in 1990.[1] This finding
was rapidly followed by demonstration of electrolumines-
cence (EL), crucial for optoelectronic utilization.[2] Because
of the domination of silicon in the electronics industry,
porous silicon could be integrated with existing technology
with relative facility.[3] In spite of 8 years of focused
investigation of this material, commercial applications
utilizing the optical or electronic capabilities of porous
silicon are still lacking. While it is readily and easily prepared
through either an electrochemical or a chemical etch, the
resulting silicon-hydride-terminated surfaces are only meta-
stable with respect to oxidation in ambient conditions.[4]
Oxidation of porous silicon in air commences in only a few
hours, leading to continuous changes in the electronic and
surface properties of the material with respect to time.
Porous silicon has a complex nanoscale architecture made
up of one-dimensional nanowires and zero-dimensional
nanocrystallites.[5] While heavily debated in the literature,[6]
it is generally accepted that PL and EL arise from quantum
confinement effects in structures smaller than 5 nm in at
least one dimension.[5] Surface states are, however, of
dramatic importance since a substantial fraction of the
silicon atoms in these small features reside on or near the
surface.[4] Surface modification can influence the boundary
conditions of the nanowires and nanocrystallites and thus the
EL and PL. These important effects remain poorly under-
stood and are a tantalizing challenge to surface chemists in
the field.
Because passivation of single-crystal silicon surfaces with
an organic overlayer bound through silicon±carbon bonds
has shown increased resistance to oxidation,[7] there has been
directed effort over the past 2±3 years towards formation of
silicon±carbon bonds on porous silicon. The majority of the
single-crystal reactions involve heating (which could poten-
tially damagethe fragile nanoscale architecture of the porous
silicon), use of late transition metals (which contaminate the
surface), or high vacuum conditions. Recently, several
groups have successfully investigated the formation of
silicon±carbon bonds on porous silicon at room temperature
through one- or two-step wet chemical techniques at room
temperature.[8±12] Examples of the different terminal groups
prepared through these chemical strategies are given in
Figure 1.
In 1996, Chazalviel and co-workers found that a silicon-
methyl-terminated surface was accessible through treatment
of the native Si±H surface of porous silicon with a methyl
Grignard reagent or methyl lithium organometallic under
anodic conditions.[8] According to the mechanism proposed
by Chazalviel, silicon hydride groups are consumed through
a transmetallation reaction, forming silicon-methyl groups
and either MgHX or LiH as by-products of the reaction.
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 3
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/0302-0265 $ 17.50+.50/0

±
[*] Prof. J. M. Buriak
Department of Chemistry
Purdue University
West Lafayette, IN 47907-1393 (USA)
[**] Financial support from Purdue University, the Purdue Research
Foundation, and the Camille and Henry Dreyfus Foundation in the
form of a New Faculty Award is gratefully acknowledged.
Fig. 1. Representative porous silicon surface functionalities prepared through
addition of an alkyl or aryl organometallic or through hydrosilylation of
alkynes or alkenes.
 266
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/0302-0266 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 3
They reported that the stability of the surfaces increased by
an order of magnitude with respect to accelerated air
oxidation at 100 C.
The intriguing result of Chazalviel and co-workers was
then pursued by the groups of Sailor[9] and Laibinis,[10] who
investigated the effects of Grignard and alkyl and aryl
lithium reagents in the absence of an electronic bias. These
groups extended the range of alkyl and aryl nucleophiles that
could be bound to the surface of porous silicon through Si±C
bonds and carried out detailed mechanistic studies. Accord-
ing to their mechanism, shown in Figure 2a, the carbanion
nucleophile attacks the weak Si±Si bond (Si±Si 215±
250 kJ mol±1, Si±H 323 kJ mol±1),[13] forming the silicon±
carbon bond and a silyl anion. The resulting silyl anion can be
further reacted with an electrophile, offering the possibility
of forming mixed surfaces. A representative selection of
some of the chemical groups bound to the surface through
this reaction scheme is shown in Figure 1. The work carried
out by these groups clearly indicates the vital effect of surface
functionalities on the photoluminescence of porous silicon.
Conjugated aromatic alkynes[9] almost quantitatively
quench the photoluminescence, whereas methyl,[8] phenyl,[9]
and 4-fluorophenyl[10] have only minor quenching effects.
A hydrosilylation approach towards formation of silicon
carbon bonds on porous silicon has been taken by our group
as outlined in Figure 2b.[11] Through the Lewis-acid-
mediated insertion of either an alkyne or an alkene into
the surface silicon±hydride bonds, efficient formation of
silicon±carbon bonds takes place.[14] Alkynes and alkenes
yield surface-bound vinyl and alkyl groups, respectively. The
reaction with alkynes is rapid, taking place within minutes;
alkenes react on the order of hours. The Lewis acid is
remarkably tolerant of a wide variety of chemical function-
alities, such as hydroxy, cyano, and nitrile groups, if an excess
with respect to alkene/alkyne is added (Fig. 1). This method
thus represents a simple and direct route to a broad range of
derivatized surfaces, which will allow for tailoring of surface
properties. Hydrophobic surfaces prepared through this
route are remarkably stable to boiling alkali solutions,
indicating that the degree of coverage is sufficient to fully
protect the exposed surface; Si±H-terminated porous silicon
dissolves in minutes under these conditions. The rate of
oxidation of 1-dodecyl-functionalized surfaces (formed from
hydrosilylation of 1-dodecene) in ambient is also substan-
tially decelerated.[15]
Very recently, we have found that hydrosilylation of a
variety of alkenes and alkynes can be induced by simple
white-light illumination (Figs. 2b and 3) with intensities in
the range of 22±44 mW cm±2.[12] 1-Dodecene and 1-dodecyne
hydrosilylation on porous silicon produces surfaces in which
97 % and 61 % of their original photoluminescence is
preserved, respectively. These two surfaces are, in addition,
stable to aerated boiling KOH (pH 10) solution. The light-
induced reaction mechanism is difficult to ascertain but may
involve cleavage of weak Si±H bonds to form Si radicals,[16]
Research News
Fig. 2. a) Proposed reaction mechanism for addition of an alkyl or aryl
organometallic to silicon-hydride-terminated porous silicon surfaces (no bias).
b) Hydrosilylation of alkynes and alkenes on silicon-hydride-terminated
porous silicon. The reaction can be either mediated by EtAlCl2, a Lewis acid,
or induced by white light illumination.
Fig. 3. Photograph of the photoluminescence of a photopatterned porous
silicon surface (12 mm diameter) upon irradiation with a 365 nm hand-held
UV lamp. The darker areas have been reacted with 1-dodecyne, yielding a 1-
dodecenyl-terminated surface (the photopattern). The lighter areas are
unfunctionalized, native, Si±H-terminated porours silicon. The pattern
appears darker since the photoluminescence is quenched by ~40 %.

variety of alkenes and alkynes can be induced by simple

the range of 22±44 mW cm±2.[12] 1-Dodecene and 1-dodecyne
hydrosilylation on porous silicon produces surfaces in which
97 % and 61 % of their original photoluminescence is
preserved, respectively. These two surfaces are, in addition,
stable to aerated boiling KOH (pH 10) solution. The light-
induced reaction mechanism is difficult to ascertain but may
involve cleavage of weak Si±H bonds to form Si radicals,[16]
Fig. 2. a) Proposed reaction mechanism for addition of an alkyl or aryl
organometallic to silicon-hydride-terminated porous silicon surfaces (no bias).
b) Hydrosilylation of alkynes and alkenes on silicon-hydride-terminated
porous silicon. The reaction can be either mediated by EtAlCl2, a Lewis acid,
or induced by white light illumination.
Fig. 3. Photograph of the photoluminescence of a photopatterned porous
silicon surface (12 mm diameter) upon irradiation with a 365 nm hand-held
UV lamp. The darker areas have been reacted with 1-dodecyne, yielding a 1-

or attack by the alkene or alkyne nucleophile of holes that
have migrated to the silicon surface.[17] In any case, the
Lewis-acid-mediated and white-light-induced hydrosilyla-
tion reactions are complementary with respect to their
functional group compatibility. For instance, hydrosilylation
of 1H,1H,2H-perfluorodecene can be smoothly carried out
using the white light procedure. Perfluoroalkyls, however,
are incompatible with the Lewis acid, EtAlCl2, and thus
hydrosilylation cannot be achieved through this method. The
light chemistry, on the other hand, is unsuccessful if the
alkene or alkyne contains a nucleophile such as hydroxy or
nitrile substituents, which are tolerated in the Lewis acid
Because hydrosilylation is light directed, the possibility of
photopatterning exists. Photopatterned hydrosilylation of 1-
dodecyne to produce a dodecenyl-terminated surface is
shown in Figure 3. Since the reaction takes place only in the
illuminated areas of the porous silicon, the masked areas (Si±
H terminated) can then be reacted with a different alkene or
alkyne to produce spatially defined areas of specific chemical
groups. This feature could be useful for design of multi-
purpose sensors or lithographic applications, for example.
The investigations outlined here provide a strong indica-
tion of the ease and usefulness of silicon±carbon bond
formation on porous silicon. Through this series of com-
plementary reactions, the scope of chemical functionalities
that may be incorporated onto porous silicon is broad and
diverse. The increased stability of the material terminated
with silicon±carbon bonds is extremely promising for future
Note added in proof: Thermal hydrosilylation of alkenes
and alkynes on porous silicon was recently reported. [18]
